BenGoZen: Or draws me to the yin yang?
C[BenGoZen: I think what draws me to go is how complex the game can be when you consider how simple the premise is.
BenGoZen: After all, it seems to be simply black and white stones and connects dots.
BenGoZen: But the psychology, the battles, the back and forth
BenGoZen: And the ability to actually measure your strength is probably what has kept me addicted to this game for so long.
BenGoZen: In terms of yin yang, I think it's an attraction to the notion of balance.
BenGoZen: And it is a symbol that reminds me of go and vice versa due to the very obvious symbolized connection of the black and white pieces.

C[BenGoZen: In terms of starting, I recommend going to a place where a lot of people come and go: like a coffee shop or something.
BenGoZen: Then go ahead and pull out your board and start reviewing a game on the board.
BenGoZen: Over time people will see you playing, and will probably be curious.
BenGoZen: And then you have one of two choices
BenGoZen: Any time you see someone look over, you can ask them if they'd like to learn. In that case, I would have a 9x9 board ready to do so.
BenGoZen: Or, you can put up a little sign / paper that welcomes people to interrupt you and learn about the game,.
BenGoZen: My experience is primarily at festivals like China or Japan Festivals where people are already interested to learn about the culture, so it's very easy to get people to sit down.
BenGoZen: When I've had more experience doing it in random places though, I'll be sure to let you know.
BenGoZen: To your second question, when it comes to teaching games, don't worry about undo.
BenGoZen: You want to let them undo as much as they want because that means they are learning.
BenGoZen: And because you are not trying to win, it shouldn't matter to you anyways. Haha.
BenGoZen: Only when they start wanting to play serious games and get stronger will undo matter.
BenGoZen: But otherwise, if it's for fun, let them undo as much as they want. =D

column1: Forensicator: The ""game of stones"" guy is a great example of what I don't want to do, and the reason why I'm planning before I just start blogging.

Forensicator: I've thought of a series I'd love to try, something like ""Fuseki decoded,"" where I focus really heavily on the first fifteen or so moves of the game, and I go through the full game to show how each of the fifteen ended up affecting the ending position.
Forensicator: Obviously it couldn't be a perfect decoding, as that would involve unparalleled reading skill, but it seems like a fun task to track as I grow as a player.
Forensicator: As one last side note before I stop being so dang chatty, I noticed that your book review section of the site is fairly small :( Ever plan on revisiting it?

BenGoZen: It's a big item on my to do list to revisit the entire design, layout, and presentation of all the reviews on the blog: books and equipment alike.
BenGoZen: So I'm hoping that sooner rather than later, I'll be able to bring my vision into fruition.

Forensicator: May I ask why? (Forgive me, I'm treating this like a teaching game). Here's how I see the board:

Forensicator: Black has cash in the bottom right but that position can be invaded if white gets strong, and it seems unable to build itself without strenghtening white elsewhere. The black group at R9 is safe for now, but can get put under attack at a moment's notice and can't strengthen itself without hurting another group. The black wall at Q15 might be able to threaten an invasion against the K16 stones, but that will be very tricky and is unlikely given the gap in our skill level. There are no points for black on the right aside from eyes. On the left, Black has some cash at the C11 group, but white has much more cash with the corner at D4, which can be invaded but which can use strength from the invasion to severely reduce the K3 group. At the top, the E17 stones are pretty much done for, and W has tons of points up top. No points in the middle.
Forensicator: R12 isn't alive, but it sure as heck isn't dead.
Forensicator: So, as I see it, I need to capture a group to win, but you are playing quite solidly.

Forensicator: So right now, I'm thinking that because I need to invade or capture, I need to strengthen my weaker groups in sente. THe question is whether that's the Q14 group or the R8 group. My thinking is, if I strengthen Q14 and you respond by leaning on R9, R9 may strengthen and hurt R4. So Q14 is the part to strengthen.

Forensicator: I'm wondering about 3-3 invasion after this making it overconcentrated, but I think I can use that to build up intolerable influence for an invasion at the top, meaning that you won't do it.

C[BenGoZen: So as you can see, this ended up being kind of a trade-off.
BenGoZen: Instead of trying to actively kill you, I switched directions and went for the corner instead.
BenGoZen: Early on when I started playing, I looked at invasions as something to kill.
BenGoZen: But oftentimes they are simply opportunities to switch priorities.
BenGoZen: Trying to kill invasions is typically impossible (unless it's a super overplay)
BenGoZen: You'll see strong players invade all kinds of crazy places and magically dance their way out of dying.
